posts.title: 1 in 16 documents flagged as fraudulent: Inside our 2026 report
posts.author: Brianna Valleskey
posts.description: Inscribe's 2026 report reveals that approximately 6% of documents processed showed signs of fraud, with AI-generated document fraud increasing nearly fivefold in 2025. The analysis examines emerging threats, detection strategies, and best practices from leading fraud prevention teams across financial institutions.

posts.title: Document Fraud Stories: Real Cases That Show Why Detection Can't Wait
posts.author: Brianna Valleskey
posts.description: Real-world document fraud cases from 2024-2026 including the Graceland forgery scheme and a $650 million Ponzi scheme built on fake entertainment contracts. Emphasizes that fraudulent documents are typically caught through contextual analysis recognizing metadata anomalies and semantic inconsistencies.

posts.title: Meet the AI Assistant helping teams investigate document fraud faster
posts.author: Stephanie Spangler
posts.description: Inscribe has introduced an AI Assistant that functions as a conversational co-pilot within its fraud detection platform, enabling risk teams to quickly investigate suspicious documents and explain fraud signals without switching between applications.

posts.title: What "Explainable AI" actually means and how to tell if a fraud vendor has it
posts.author: Conor Burke
posts.description: Examines why transparency in AI fraud detection systems matters for financial institutions, arguing that genuine explainability means showing reasoning step-by-step. Offers four key questions organizations should ask vendors about audit-ready documentation and human oversight.

posts.title: Inscribe and Taktile launch embedded document fraud detection integration
posts.author: Stephanie Spangler
posts.description: Inscribe has partnered with Taktile to integrate agentic document fraud detection directly into their decision platform, enabling banks and lenders to identify suspicious documents automatically within existing workflows.
